steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Text Structure and Purpose question. The poem is from Paul Laurence Dunbar's "The Poet and His Song." The question asks for the main purpose of the text — what the poem is primarily doing or communicating overall.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'To convey how engaging in song makes the speaker feel': The poem opens "A song is but a little thing, / And yet what joy it is to sing!" and goes on to show how singing gives the speaker zest during toil, comfort at day's end, and peace ("I sing my song, and all is well"). The entire poem traces the emotional effect of singing on the speaker. ✓
  • Choice B — 'To compare the speaker to a singing shepherd': "Night, the shepherd" is a metaphor for night herding stars — it is not a comparison between the speaker and a shepherd. The speaker sings; Night is the shepherd. ✗
  • Choice C — 'To portray the speaker's excitement about farming': Farming imagery (cows, fold, bell) provides the setting, but the poem is about singing in that setting, not excitement about farm life. ✗
  • Choice D — 'To describe the pieces of music the speaker enjoys hearing': The speaker is singing, not listening to others. The poem describes the act and effect of the speaker's own singing, not pieces of music enjoyed by listening. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
